Replying to email, setting up example Module structure

cards.desc:
Hi Peter,

‌

Thanks for the update, please see my replies inline with your original email below.

‌

Kindly,

‌

**Rich Bartlett**
Learning Designer 

**Online Programs Team | Learning Enhancement & Innovation**
Division of Academic and Student Engagement

‌

**THE UNIVERSITY OF ADELAIDE**
Room 661, Level 6, Kenneth Wills Building
T: [+61 0467 982 427](tel:+61467982427 "‌") 
E: [rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au](mailto:rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au "‌")

Cricos Provider Number 00123M

![Logo

Description automatically generated](cid:2378437861*image001.gif@01DB6298.D27510C0)

‌

‌

**From:** Peter Pudney <[Peter.Pudney@unisa.edu.au](mailto:Peter.Pudney@unisa.edu.au "‌")\>
**Date:** Wednesday, 8 January 2025 at 10:57 pm
**To:** Rich Bartlett <[rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au](mailto:rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au "‌")\>
**Subject:** AU: Mathematics Clinic 1

Rich,

Here is a bunch of questions for you, and an update on what I have been doing.

‌

\# Modules

I have discussed the module:topic structure for Mathematics Clinic with ATP Jorge Aarao, who is familiar with the Clinic at UniSA. He is happy with my proposed structure, which at the moment has a single page for each of the main phases of the project:

Great, thanks for this – It just covers our bases for when the course is audited we can stand tall saying the format/structure has been approved.

I appreciated you don’t necessary need those “concept pages” as you’ve covered everything in the preceding page. That said, I’ve kept the master heading and nestled your working pages underneath. Like so.

‌

‌

- Getting started/Welcome (draft completed)

![Image](cid:2378437861*image002.png@01DB6299.A194E650)

I’ve tweaked some of the formatting and updated the page with some spelling and grammatical improvements. Instead of highlighting changes, I think it would be more efficient to simply skim read the page. If there’s any changes you don’t agree with please feel free to revert to edit your liking.

I’ve added a “Show References” accordion at the bottom of the page (as will do this for future pages if required) please review the APA 7 referencing. 

‌

- Weekly Technical Reports (draft completed)

I’ve replied to your \@ question on the page and applied some formatting on the lower half of the page. Please take a look.

- Work Statement (draft completed, but there is a formatting question for you)

I imagine the Maths\_Clinic\_2024\_Trapeze\_Work_Statement.pdf & Work Statement Cover Letter.pdf will need to be reformatted to AU style. Would you like me to ask the media team to action this now?

- Making progress (to be done)
- Midyear Presentations (to be done, but I have linked to an example presentation)

Again, would you like Maths\_Clinic\_2024\_Trapeze\_Midyear_Presentation.pdf to be reformatting into AU style by the media team?

‌

- Midyear Report (to be done, but I have linked to an example report).

Also, Maths\_Clinic\_2024\_Trapeze\_Midyear_Report.pdf to be updated by media team?

Each of these pages has material on it that should be read before each phase of the project commences. Each phase will last multiple weeks, the phases will overlap, and at any time different students will be working on different things, so there is not a fixed mapping from topics to weeks.

That’s ok, if that’s the flow and process of the clinic then so be it.

If you think there is too much information on each page (for students viewing material on mobile phones, for example) then I am happy for the content for each module to be split over multiple pages. But I think it is better to scroll on one page, since the students should read all the information for each phase before they start the phase. The students will receive further guidance from the academic advisors during the weekly team meetings.

I think the amount of wording the page is fine, that said, I wonder if it’s worth putting example work statements, presentations, reports on their own pages and providing further framing text to unpack the examples and guide students in a little more detail. (Opposed to just saying “here’s an example”) If you think unnecessary please say.

The Weekly Technical Reports module has an Events page, but there is not much on it. I could also add Events for submitting the Work Statement, Midyear Presentations and Midyear Reports. What do you think? Is it worth putting these in to remind the students of deadlines for the major deliverables? Or is the Assessment Overview (from CurV) enough?

I think it’s worth adding these pages as an extra reminder for students. I don’t know what the CurV pages will look like because the LMS and CurV aren’t connected yet. So I would suggest add information that will guide students on pages we can see and control.

In the Home page, the Course Content box has “Work Statement” before “Weekly Technical Reports”. Can you swap these around for me, so that “Weekly Technical Reports” comes first?

Done

If you are happy with the structure then I will delete the pages I am not using.

No worries, FYI I’ve already deleted a bunch of pages for you, like concept and summary pages.

\# Discussion forums

Currently, we let students in each group choose their own preferred methods of communicating amongst themselves. We don’t see these discussions. But the students do report weekly on what they have been doing, we talk to each Project Manager weekly, and the students reach out to the academics when they need to.

Does Canvas have a facility that will support student discussions within each project group, and is easier to use than WhatsApp or whatever the students are using now?

I can confirm that Canvas LMS has Discussion forms with the ability to create groups and assign specific people to each group> [https://community.canvaslms.com/t5/Instructor-Guide/How-do-I-create-a-group-discussion-in-a-course/ta-p/911](https://community.canvaslms.com/t5/Instructor-Guide/How-do-I-create-a-group-discussion-in-a-course/ta-p/911 "smartCard-inline") . I can only advise you use this as it’s within the tools approved by the university. How students communicate ad hoc will occur naturally but I don’t think we can recommend external tools.

\# Shared data storage

At UniSA, we set up a shared data space for each project using Research Data Storage. Each project team uses this for sharing all of the data, code, Weekly Technical Reports, and so on associated with the project. It is a bit like OneDrive or SharePoint—student and academic advisor machines automatically synchronise to the shared repository. Will AU have something like this?

Yes, I understand that students will have a Microsoft account so access to OneDrive will be possible.

\# Graded assessments

I am not sure what is required here. We need some way to enter grades for students, but at UniSA the students don’t submit anything—their individual Weekly Technical Reports are stored in the shared repository where everyone in the team can see them, and the group reports are on-line documents that are shared with the academic advisors and Clinic Director.

I might circle back to \@Jamie Nemorin here to confirm what you and Jamie discussed in your time together. If you need to enter grades then we will need to create pages within this “[assignments](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1936/assignments "‌")” page. Jamie, was the plan to use a template unique to this type of course, are we using rubrics? Can you please provide any insights into what we might do here? 

\# Peer assessment

I need to set up some form of peer assessment, where students can assess themselves and other students in their group. What is the mechanism for this? The peer assessments are used to moderate individual scores for group work.

There is a tool being approved and implemented in the next few months called [Feedback Fruits](https://feedbackfruits.com/ "‌"). With FeedbackFruits Peer Review, students annotate and provide feedback on each other’s assignments. This improves students’ assignment skills, as well as their skills at giving, receiving and using constructive feedback. Students can use FeedbackFruits Peer Review to annotate each other’s documents (like you do in SpeedGrader), as well as grade each other using the assignment rubric. If you like, this feedback can be anonymous.

As the tool isn’t available to us yet, we can create some placeholder information that could then be easily imported in FF once up and running. I’ve asked a colleague to provide some guidance on what could be included in the placeholder text so I’ll come back to you.

I would suggest having a look at the links below, but I imagine we would need to consult with some colleague in the **Capability Development Team** who are well verses in this tool and ask for this assistance in set up and tweaking to the unique structure of the clinics.

- This [AU workshop _Creating assessments_](https://adelaide.au.panopto.com/Panopto/Pages/Viewer.aspx?pid=8022e73e-f673-43cf-855d-b1c300231a04&id=0682684d-aed5-4ea5-a782-b23d0034fb97&advance=true "‌") has a broad overview of FF in general from 35:12 to 43:07. This is both GME and PR.
- This [UoA workshop for Peer Review](https://myuni.adelaide.edu.au/courses/90507/pages/use-a-feedbackfruits-peer-review-assignment "‌") has recordings and slides available. The slides don't mention how to use PR - they mostly cover pedagogy and why FF PR should be chosen over other peer review methods.
  Peter
cards.name: Replying to updates

cards.desc:
Hi Georgina,

‌

Thanks for taking the time to review the prototype and for your feedback. I completely understand your concerns about the branching simulation not fully capturing the nuance needed for a social science analysis—happy to drop that idea and pivot to other interactive approaches if/as required.

‌

Great to hear that you’re nearly done with organising the weekly readings. Just checking, I noticed you have the following reading on the Topic 1 [concept page](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/2114/pages/cultural-ecology-and-cultural-landscapes "‌"), however, it doesn’t seem to appear in the Course Readings List.

_Baer, Hans. A. 2008. “Toward a Critical Anthropology on the Impact of Global Warming on Health and Human Societies.” Medical Anthropology 27(1): 2-8. _[_https://doi.org/10.1080/01459740701831369Links_](https://doi.org/10.1080/01459740701831369Links "‌")_ to an external site._

‌

‌

![Image](cid:1014873164*image005.png@01DB788B.CC473E90)

‌

You may also noticed I’ve “wrapped” this reading in a blue direction box. This is a standard practice we’re using as part of the new AU style guide. Are you happy for me to do this for all your readings on relevant pages?

‌

‌

‌

Once Jaye is back and working on the video- and reading-based quizzes, I’ll be happy to assist as needed in Weeks 7-8 while I’m still available. Since she’ll be handling the reading summaries and PowerPoints, that sounds like a good way to ensure key concepts are reinforced in the quizzes.

![Image](cid:1014873164*image006.png@01DB788B.E74D1F20)

‌

It’s also great to hear that you’ve got the videos in place for each topic and the asynchronous pages. 

‌

For the interactive seminar pages, would you like me to wrap the wording in an “activity box” similar to the reading box above? In the same task, I’m happy to help with stepping out instructions and implementing additional structure so that all students benefit from the guidance you undoubtedly provide. This will ensure all students are on the same page and there’s consistency throughout the course pages.

I can simply provide structure to the text with relevant headings, etc, and then you’re more than welcome to edit/tweak as you see fit. 

‌

Please see this page> [https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/2114/pages/interactive-seminar?module\_item\_id=78330](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/2114/pages/interactive-seminar?module_item_id=78330 "‌")

Mitzi Jozeps, a Project Officer within the Curriculum team at the Integration Management Office (IMO), introduces herself as the key contact for the development of the Adelaide University Mathematics Clinic 1 course. She outlines her role in supporting the 10-week course development process, including access to resources, periodic check-ins, and guidance via the Course Developer Journey.

The email provides:

1. **Key contacts**: Roles and responsibilities, time allocations, and availability of Curriculum Developers, Learning Designers, Media Design and Production, and Library support.
2. **Next steps**: Links to essential resources, including the Course Development Essentials site, course shell, and SharePoint folder.
3. **Training and support**: Access to live and asynchronous training, workshops, and library assistance.
4. **Milestone meetings**: Scheduled check-ins at specific points during the development timeline.

Mitzi encourages Peter to review all information, ensure access to links, and attend training sessions to facilitate a successful course build. The kick-off meeting is scheduled for the week of 18 November 2024.

cards.name: Overview

cards.desc:
Hi Peter,

‌

Thanks for the update, please see my replies inline with your original email below.

‌

Kindly,

‌

**Rich Bartlett**
Learning Designer 

**Online Programs Team | Learning Enhancement & Innovation**
Division of Academic and Student Engagement

‌

**THE UNIVERSITY OF ADELAIDE**
Room 661, Level 6, Kenneth Wills Building
T: [+61 0467 982 427](tel:+61467982427 "‌") 
E: [rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au](mailto:rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au "‌")

Cricos Provider Number 00123M

![Logo

Description automatically generated](cid:2378437861*image001.gif@01DB6298.D27510C0)

‌

‌

**From:** Peter Pudney <[Peter.Pudney@unisa.edu.au](mailto:Peter.Pudney@unisa.edu.au "‌")\>
**Date:** Wednesday, 8 January 2025 at 10:57 pm
**To:** Rich Bartlett <[rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au](mailto:rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au "‌")\>
**Subject:** AU: Mathematics Clinic 1

Rich,

Here is a bunch of questions for you, and an update on what I have been doing.

‌

\# Modules

I have discussed the module:topic structure for Mathematics Clinic with ATP Jorge Aarao, who is familiar with the Clinic at UniSA. He is happy with my proposed structure, which at the moment has a single page for each of the main phases of the project:

Great, thanks for this – It just covers our bases for when the course is audited we can stand tall saying the format/structure has been approved.

I appreciated you don’t necessary need those “concept pages” as you’ve covered everything in the preceding page. That said, I’ve kept the master heading and nestled your working pages underneath. Like so.

‌

‌

- Getting started/Welcome (draft completed)

![Image](cid:2378437861*image002.png@01DB6299.A194E650)

I’ve tweaked some of the formatting and updated the page with some spelling and grammatical improvements. Instead of highlighting changes, I think it would be more efficient to simply skim read the page. If there’s any changes you don’t agree with please feel free to revert to edit your liking.

I’ve added a “Show References” accordion at the bottom of the page (as will do this for future pages if required) please review the APA 7 referencing. 

‌

- Weekly Technical Reports (draft completed)

I’ve replied to your \@ question on the page and applied some formatting on the lower half of the page. Please take a look.

- Work Statement (draft completed, but there is a formatting question for you)

I imagine the Maths\_Clinic\_2024\_Trapeze\_Work_Statement.pdf & Work Statement Cover Letter.pdf will need to be reformatted to AU style. Would you like me to ask the media team to action this now?

- Making progress (to be done)
- Midyear Presentations (to be done, but I have linked to an example presentation)

Again, would you like Maths\_Clinic\_2024\_Trapeze\_Midyear_Presentation.pdf to be reformatting into AU style by the media team?

‌

- Midyear Report (to be done, but I have linked to an example report).

Also, Maths\_Clinic\_2024\_Trapeze\_Midyear_Report.pdf to be updated by media team?

Each of these pages has material on it that should be read before each phase of the project commences. Each phase will last multiple weeks, the phases will overlap, and at any time different students will be working on different things, so there is not a fixed mapping from topics to weeks.

That’s ok, if that’s the flow and process of the clinic then so be it.

If you think there is too much information on each page (for students viewing material on mobile phones, for example) then I am happy for the content for each module to be split over multiple pages. But I think it is better to scroll on one page, since the students should read all the information for each phase before they start the phase. The students will receive further guidance from the academic advisors during the weekly team meetings.

I think the amount of wording the page is fine, that said, I wonder if it’s worth putting example work statements, presentations, reports on their own pages and providing further framing text to unpack the examples and guide students in a little more detail. (Opposed to just saying “here’s an example”) If you think unnecessary please say.

The Weekly Technical Reports module has an Events page, but there is not much on it. I could also add Events for submitting the Work Statement, Midyear Presentations and Midyear Reports. What do you think? Is it worth putting these in to remind the students of deadlines for the major deliverables? Or is the Assessment Overview (from CurV) enough?

I think it’s worth adding these pages as an extra reminder for students. I don’t know what the CurV pages will look like because the LMS and CurV aren’t connected yet. So I would suggest add information that will guide students on pages we can see and control.

In the Home page, the Course Content box has “Work Statement” before “Weekly Technical Reports”. Can you swap these around for me, so that “Weekly Technical Reports” comes first?

Done

If you are happy with the structure then I will delete the pages I am not using.

No worries, FYI I’ve already deleted a bunch of pages for you, like concept and summary pages.

\# Discussion forums

Currently, we let students in each group choose their own preferred methods of communicating amongst themselves. We don’t see these discussions. But the students do report weekly on what they have been doing, we talk to each Project Manager weekly, and the students reach out to the academics when they need to.

Does Canvas have a facility that will support student discussions within each project group, and is easier to use than WhatsApp or whatever the students are using now?

I can confirm that Canvas LMS has Discussion forms with the ability to create groups and assign specific people to each group> [https://community.canvaslms.com/t5/Instructor-Guide/How-do-I-create-a-group-discussion-in-a-course/ta-p/911](https://community.canvaslms.com/t5/Instructor-Guide/How-do-I-create-a-group-discussion-in-a-course/ta-p/911 "smartCard-inline") . I can only advise you use this as it’s within the tools approved by the university. How students communicate ad hoc will occur naturally but I don’t think we can recommend external tools.

\# Shared data storage

At UniSA, we set up a shared data space for each project using Research Data Storage. Each project team uses this for sharing all of the data, code, Weekly Technical Reports, and so on associated with the project. It is a bit like OneDrive or SharePoint—student and academic advisor machines automatically synchronise to the shared repository. Will AU have something like this?

Yes, I understand that students will have a Microsoft account so access to OneDrive will be possible.

\# Graded assessments

I am not sure what is required here. We need some way to enter grades for students, but at UniSA the students don’t submit anything—their individual Weekly Technical Reports are stored in the shared repository where everyone in the team can see them, and the group reports are on-line documents that are shared with the academic advisors and Clinic Director.

I might circle back to \@Jamie Nemorin here to confirm what you and Jamie discussed in your time together. If you need to enter grades then we will need to create pages within this “[assignments](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1936/assignments "‌")” page. Jamie, was the plan to use a template unique to this type of course, are we using rubrics? Can you please provide any insights into what we might do here? 

\# Peer assessment

I need to set up some form of peer assessment, where students can assess themselves and other students in their group. What is the mechanism for this? The peer assessments are used to moderate individual scores for group work.

There is a tool being approved and implemented in the next few months called [Feedback Fruits](https://feedbackfruits.com/ "‌"). With FeedbackFruits Peer Review, students annotate and provide feedback on each other’s assignments. This improves students’ assignment skills, as well as their skills at giving, receiving and using constructive feedback. Students can use FeedbackFruits Peer Review to annotate each other’s documents (like you do in SpeedGrader), as well as grade each other using the assignment rubric. If you like, this feedback can be anonymous.

As the tool isn’t available to us yet, we can create some placeholder information that could then be easily imported in FF once up and running. I’ve asked a colleague to provide some guidance on what could be included in the placeholder text so I’ll come back to you.

I would suggest having a look at the links below, but I imagine we would need to consult with some colleague in the **Capability Development Team** who are well verses in this tool and ask for this assistance in set up and tweaking to the unique structure of the clinics.

- This [AU workshop _Creating assessments_](https://adelaide.au.panopto.com/Panopto/Pages/Viewer.aspx?pid=8022e73e-f673-43cf-855d-b1c300231a04&id=0682684d-aed5-4ea5-a782-b23d0034fb97&advance=true "‌") has a broad overview of FF in general from 35:12 to 43:07. This is both GME and PR.
- This [UoA workshop for Peer Review](https://myuni.adelaide.edu.au/courses/90507/pages/use-a-feedbackfruits-peer-review-assignment "‌") has recordings and slides available. The slides don't mention how to use PR - they mostly cover pedagogy and why FF PR should be chosen over other peer review methods.
  Peter

This email details the 10-week course development process for the Adelaide University course _Passions and Interests: The History of Greed_, scheduled from 18 November 2024 to 7 February 2025. Sharon Mychajliw, Project Officer, will serve as the primary contact, providing resources, guidance, and periodic check-ins.

**Key Support Contacts:**

- **Curriculum & Education Developer (Corinne Green):** Advises on course planning, assessments, and structure (5 hours, Weeks 2-5).
- **Learning Designer (Richard Bartlett):** Supports media and activity design (15 hours, Weeks 4-8) and coordinates LMS course build (20 hours, Weeks 6-10).
- **Media Design and Production:** Provides guidance on media selection and assists with content creation (Media Design - 5 hours from Weeks 4-10; Media Production - 15 hours from Weeks 6-10).
- **Library Support:** Helps with selecting readings and copyright guidance (Weeks 2-4, as needed).

**Resources and Training:**

- **Course Development Essentials Site:** Includes guidance on the phases of design, development, and build, along with templates and the Adelaide Attainment Model.
- **Course Shell and SharePoint Folder:** Spaces for course content creation and collaborative document storage.
- **Training Hub and Workshops:** Offers live training in Weeks 2, 3, 5, and 7, with workshops on Canvas, media, and assessment design.

**Milestone Check-ins:**
Scheduled meetings are set for:

- Week 1: Kick-off (w/c 18 Nov)
- Week 4: Assessment and course design (w/c 9 Dec)
- Week 7: Course development progress (w/c 13 Jan)
- Week 10: Course completion (w/c 3 Feb)

Sharon encourages participation in training and workshops, noting any absences that may affect milestone meetings.

cards.name: Overview

cards.desc:
Hi Weitong,

Thank you for reaching out. I’m happy to assist with enhancing the course layout and visual design. Below are my responses and suggestions for each aspect you’ve outlined:

**Layout design**

I can help uplift the layout by ensuring the correct use of heading structures (H1, H2, H3, H4, etc.) to improve navigation and accessibility for students. Additionally, I recommend implementing **direction/cue boxes** for wrapping text, such as suggested readings. These will help frame students' attention and make navigating the page more intuitive.

‌

See example:

‌

‌

‌

**Graphic polishing**

You’ve provided a link to all the images in the course, which is helpful. Upon scanning through Session 1, I noticed that many images appear clear and accessible, so they likely don’t require attention. Would you prefer I:

1. Use my judgement to identify which images need to go to the media team,
2. Or would you like to provide me with a specific list of images requiring redesign?

‌

This will help streamline the process and ensure efficient use of resources.

‌

**Module and topic naming conventions**

‌

1. To align with the **Course Development Essentials** site, the "Modules" page boxes need to follow this structure:

‌

[Module name]: [Topic # - Topic name].

‌

Then each “concept page” is essentially a name of sub topic

‌

In your course, instead of "Session 1, 2, 3", the boxes should reflect a curriculum module name followed by the topic name. Let me know if you’d like assistance updating these labels.

‌

1. I believe all of the Tutorials, being “Key events’ should be grouped under this heading. I’m sure you have some reasoning in terms of students’ sequence and engagement as to why there’s a tutorial after each concept page. I don’t think it’s necessarily incorrect, just that it doesn’t align with the ‘conventions’ of the AU course designs.

‌

1. Course Delivery Model: Your course appears to be structured from Session 1 to Session 4. I’m keen to understand and potentially help you align this to the Modular Curriculum Model as per the info on the [Course Development Essentials site](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/370/pages/course-delivery-model/ "‌"). This may involve organising content into modular blocks to support flexibility and reflect the future **modified semester format** (10 teaching weeks + 3 extension weeks). If you’ve factored this in already, please let me know and perhaps a course map needs to be developed to represent how what you are naming the sessions fits into this format. Please **see PDF attached** for screenshot of a 10 topic/week course.

‌

Here’s an example of Module: topic naming:

‌

‌

‌

‌

**LD Feedback on headings (e.g., "Questions for Students")**

More of a learning design bit of feedback On page [**1.5: Overfitting and Regularisation**](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1595/pages/1-dot-5-overfitting-and-regularization?module_item_id=60037 "‌") (for example), I noticed the "Questions for Students" section at the bottom of the page. What are students expected to do with their answers? Will they receive feedback on their responses? Clarifying this could help students better understand the purpose of these questions and engage with the content more effectively.

‌

**Further reading**

Do you have a comprehensive list of readings for the course? If so, the Library team can assist with importing these into the **Course Readings** tool within the course site. This will help centralise the materials for easy access.

‌

i.e, again on page [**1.5: Overfitting and Regularisation**](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1595/pages/1-dot-5-overfitting-and-regularization?module_item_id=60037 "‌")**,** are the following actual articles, books, journals? Ideally this text will be hyperlinked to the course reading platform or the reading itself on the web so that students can click easily to open and access it.

‌

- **Research the Double Descent Pattern**: Understand how adding more complexity can initially worsen and then improve generalization in deep learning models.
- **Explore Different Regularization Techniques**: Compare the effectiveness of **early stopping**, **weight decay**, and **dropout** on a real dataset.

‌

‌

**Rubrics**

For **Assignments (1 – 3)**, I noticed the rubric uses only four rating criteria. This doesn’t align with the recommended five-level convention (HD, D, C, P, F). You may have done this for a particular reason I’m not aware of, so just curious was this approach discussed with [\@Bopelo Boitshwarelo](mailto:Bopelo.Boitshwarelo@unisa.edu.au "‌") during the initial design stages?

‌

**Avatar-based introduction video**

I've been informed by TALAS management that an avatar-based video is not required at this stage. Instead, the **video script** provided is sufficient, and this has been prioritised for now. Videos can be recorded later as needed. (I’m only speculating, but it’s possible that avatar-based technology may be implemented in the future.)

‌

Apologies for the long email, I’d be happy to meet in person or online early next week to discuss and unpack these points.

‌

Please let me know how you’d like to proceed on these items. I’m happy to adjust my approach to align with your priorities and timelines.

‌

Kind regards,

‌

**Rich Bartlett**
Learning Designer

**Online Programs Team | Learning Enhancement & Innovation**
Division of Academic and Student Engagement

‌

**THE UNIVERSITY OF ADELAIDE**
Room 661, Level 6, Kenneth Wills Building
T: +61 0467 982 427 
E: [rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au](mailto:rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au "‌")

Cricos Provider Number 00123M

‌

‌

**From:** Weitong Chen <[weitong.chen@adelaide.edu.au](mailto:weitong.chen@adelaide.edu.au "‌")\>
**Date:** Thursday, 28 November 2024 at 12:40 pm
**To:** Rich Bartlett <[rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au](mailto:rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au "‌")\>
**Cc:** Sally Kuzniecow <[Sally.Kuzniecow@unisa.edu.au](mailto:Sally.Kuzniecow@unisa.edu.au "‌")\>
**Subject:** Request for Support on Course Website Layout and Design

Dear Reichaed,

‌

I hope this message finds you well. I am reaching out to seek your assistance in enhancing the layout and visual design of the course website for **Advanced Applied Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning**. The course is approximately **90% complete**, with the content and assessments fully developed.

‌

While the core structure and materials are in place, I believe improving the layout and graphics could significantly enhance the user experience for our students. Specifically, I would appreciate your support with:

- **Layout Design**: Ensuring the course website is intuitive, accessible, and visually appealing.
- **Graphic Polishing**: Refining the visual elements to achieve consistency and maintain student engagement.

Additionally, many of the figures ([https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1595/files/folder/fig](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1595/files/folder/fig "‌")) included in the course content are currently borrowed from the internet. To ensure a cohesive style and address copyright concerns, these figures need to be redesigned or re-created in a professional and consistent manner.

‌

Regarding the introduction video (e.g.: [https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1595/pages/topic-overview?module\_item\_id=37037](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1595/pages/topic-overview?module_item_id=37037 "‌")), the script is ready, and since I will not be delivering this course, it would be ideal to use an avatar-based video. Would you be able to help identify someone who can assist with creating and recording the video? If you have recommendations for tools or platforms that support avatar-based video creation, I’d greatly appreciate your input.

‌

Please let me know if you’re available to assist with these tasks or if additional details are needed to proceed.  Looking forward to hearing your thoughts.

‌

Kind regards,

Weitong Chen

‌

‌

**Dr Tony Weitong Chen**

Pronoun He, Him, His

‌

ARC EC Industry Fellow, Academic (AIML), DT-Lab Co-director | Senior Lecturer, SET EMCR Rep.

Australian Institute for Machine Learning (AIML) | School of Computer and Mathematical Science 

Faculty of Sciences, Engineering & Technology

The University of Adelaide, AUSTRALIA 5005

A: Room 4.45 Ingkarni Wardli building | E: [T.Chen@adelaide.edu.au](mailto:T.Chen@adelaide.edu.au "‌") | T: 08 8313 0676

ALLY Network [http://ua.edu.au/allynetwork](http://ua.edu.au/allynetwork "‌")

‌

Cricos Provider Number 00123M

‌

‌

‌

\----------------------------------------------------------

**IMPORTANT:** This message may contain confidential or legally privileged information. If you think it was sent to you by mistake, please delete all copies and advise the sender. For the purposes of the SPAM Act 2003, this email is authorised by The University of Adelaide.

cards.name: Requested help in Week 8 - Reply email

cards.desc:
Hi Weitong,

Thanks for your openness to feedback and for swiftly implementing those changes.

1. I like this navigation tool however, a system-wide navigation bar is coming, so I’ve removed this functionality on pages where it appears and all courses will be updated one the tool is enabled.

‌

‌

‌

‌

1.  Please review these pages I’ve worked on:

‌

**Layout/Design**

1. I’ve reworked [this](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1595/pages/topic-overview "‌") page to remove any duplication between the video script and the words on the page. This updated structure ensures the text-based info is concise, easy to reference, and suitable for learners seeking a clear summary after watching the video.
2. I’ve reformatted the headings for the first tutorial: [Setup Environment: Step-by-Step Guide to Setting Up a Deep Learning Environment](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1595/pages/setup-environment-step-by-step-guide-to-setting-up-a-deep-learning-environment "‌")
3. [Tutorial 1.2: Understanding Forward and Backward Propagation in Neural Networks](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1595/pages/tutorial-1-dot-2-understanding-forward-and-backward-propagation-in-neural-networks "‌"), comment at the bottom of the page

‌

‌

Apologies, I forgot to mention that each “module:topic” section is supposed to have a _**Summary**_ page. I’ve added one for you [here](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1595/modules/items/67194 "‌"). If you’re open to it, the page will need some summarising text for the topic and perhaps linking to the next topic in the sequence. I can duplicate these pages if you need or you’re welcome to do this and drag them down into their required boxes.

‌

‌

1. **LD Feedback on Headings (e.g., "Questions for Students")**

‌

Please let me know how you would like to proceed and we can apply this option to all pages where questions appear.

‌

1. One option could be to add an accordion menu that presents the questions and then with a click, the row expands and reveals answers or insights for students: See [here](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1595/pages/1-dot-1-introduction-to-multilayer-perceptrons#:~:text=Reflective%20questions%3A "‌")
2. Another option could be to build the questions into a “[custom interactive](https://mediaproduction.adelaide.edu.au/oua-interactives/#/typeReveal?act=mappingInternalResources "‌")” feedback tool. Where the students can submit their thoughts/reflections in a custom field. They then click submit, and an “exemplar” answer and then text appears. This can be helpful because it convinces the students to attempt to think deeply about the questions and formulate a response without simply clicking ahead and revealing the answer via the accordion drop-down. **Either option will need your input to provide “answer” text.**
3. We leave the questions as they stand at the moment.

‌

‌

‌

‌

‌

1. **Graphic Polishing**
   1. I’ve sent this request to the media team to uplift these images. Each course has 20 hours of media budget allocation so I’ll let you know if they come back with any feedback. i.e, if this amount of images blows out the budget.
2. **Rubrics**
   1. Thanks for doing this. I’m not an expert in rubric design and perhaps [\@Bopelo](mailto:Bopelo.Boitshwarelo@unisa.edu.au "‌") might be able to circle back on this if they have capacity. My general feedback on Assignment 1 rubric for example would be (and possibly applied to the other assignments)

‌

**Clarity and Consistency**:

1. Ensure uniform use of terminology (e.g., "HD," "High Distinction," and "Distinction" should be consistent throughout).
2. For Code Quality and Documentation, in the High Distinction criteria, consider specifying what constitutes "clean, modular, and well-commented code”
3. Clearly define each performance level with specific, measurable outcomes to avoid ambiguity.

‌

**Actionable Descriptions**:

1. Frame criteria in terms of what students should do or demonstrate (e.g., "Accurately implements forward and backward propagation, achieving >85% test accuracy " instead of "Fully functional implementation").

‌

**Avoid Redundancy**:

1. Some descriptors for lower grades (e.g., "C" or "P") repeat phrases like "poor structure." These could be streamlined for brevity and clarity.

‌

**Inclusivity in Phrasing**:

1. Replace negative phrasing like "poor performance" or "minimal" with constructive alternatives, such as "requires improvement" or "limited demonstration of skills."

‌

**Ranges**

‌

I’m not sure if you’re aware, however the rubrics has a “range” feature for points. The "ranges" feature allows for a distribution of points across each performance level within the rubric. This makes it easier to provide nuanced grading based on how closely a submission meets the criteria.

‌

‌

‌

‌

‌

1. **Higher Points in the Rubric**:

Even though the assignment contributes 15% to the total grade, the rubric can allocate a higher point value (e.g., 70 or 100 points) to provide greater granularity for fair grading, particularly at lower ranges like Pass/Fail. This ensures markers can differentiate partial achievements, provide nuanced feedback, and award proportionate scores. The LMS grade book automatically scales these points to reflect the assignment’s overall weight in the course grade.

‌

1. **Accurate Representation of Performance**:
   - With ranges enabled, assessors can award marks that accurately reflect student performance within a specific grade band. For instance, within the "Distinction" range (5.5 to >4.0 points in this example), assessors can award a score of 5.3 if a submission is close to High Distinction but not fully meeting its requirements. Similarly, a student may correctly implement part of the _**preprocessing**_ but fail to complete the _**backpropagation**_. Using a range allows the marker to assign a proportionate score, such as 30/100, which reflects their partial achievement.

‌

1. **Simplified Grade Calculations**:
   - The LMS calculates final grades by scaling the total points achieved in the rubric to the weight assigned to the assessment. For example:
     - If the rubric uses 100 points and a student achieves 85, the LMS will calculate their contribution to the course grade as 85%×15%=12.75%
     - This ensures alignment between the detailed rubric grading and the course's overall grading scheme.

‌

‌

I appreciate this is a lot of feedback so please let me know if you have any questions or require clarification.

‌

Kindly,

cards.name: Overview

cards.desc:
Dear Sally,

Thank you for reaching out and for setting up a time to discuss the **Mine Automation** course. I am available for a meeting on Thursday the 14th in the AM or PM. 

To ensure I’m interpreting the details in the [2B document correctly](https://adelaideuni.sharepoint.com/:b:/r/sites/aupcd/Engineering%20Part%203/Mining%20-%20Mine%20Automation/Resources/MINING_Mine%20Automation.pdf?csf=1&web=1&e=aqNTPH "‌"), here’s a summary of how I currently understand the course structure and assessment strategy. Could you please confirm if this aligns with Chaoshui's intentions?

**Mine Automation Course Overview**

The **Mine Automation** course, within the Master of Professional Engineering (Mining) program, aims to equip students with essential skills in digital mining technologies. Covering automation, data analytics, IoT, and digital twins, the course is designed to enhance efficiency, safety, and optimisation in mining operations.

‌

**Key Focus Areas:**

- **Automation & Data Analytics**: Introduces data-driven practices and automation fundamentals in mining.
- **AI & Machine Learning**: Emphasises predictive analytics and data visualisation.
- **Sensors & Robotics**: Covers technologies for remote and autonomous operations.
- **Digital Twin & IoT Integration**: Focuses on creating digital replicas to optimise operations.
- **Mine Simulation & Optimisation**: Teaches simulation techniques to improve mining performance.

‌

**Assessment Strategy**:

1. **In-Class Tests (50%)**
   - Three tests assessing knowledge in mine automation, machine learning, simulations, and system integration.
2. **Digital Twin Project Presentation (10%)**
   - An oral presentation demonstrating the digital twin project’s application in a mining context.
3. **Project Report (40%)**
   - A detailed report documenting the development of a digital twin system to optimise ore haulage, showcasing students’ simulation and data analysis skills.

‌

**Teaching Approach**: A blend of lectures, tutorials, project-based learning, and practical labs, with hands-on tools such as HaulSim and Vulcan, supports applied learning throughout the course.

The course appears to bridge traditional mining practices with emerging technologies, preparing students to take on leadership roles in automation and data-driven mining environments.

‌

If this understanding aligns with the course objectives, it will help us approach the design and assessment components accurately moving forward. Hopefully, some progress has been made on assessment design and course mapping. If not, this may impact our timeline and resource availability.

‌

Looking forward to your confirmation and our discussion on Thursday.

‌

Kind regards,
cards.name: Initial contact via email

cards.desc:
There will be lots of videos in the course

He understands the need to restructure and resequence content for AU instance of course
cards.name: Meeting with Jamie and Chaoshui

cards.desc:
Hi Sally,

‌

Thanks for your time this morning.

‌

As discussed, I wanted to highlight some challenges with the development of Chaoshui's course. Specifically, there seems to be a misalignment between Chaoshui's understanding of the requirements and the standards we need for course design.

‌

Here are the key issues I noticed during our meeting:

- **Page Naming Conventions**: Chaoshui uses full sentences as page names (e.g., for the Topic Overview), which are then repeated as the page content itself.
- **Modules vs Topics**: There's confusion around the naming and structure of Modules versus Topics. It also remains unclear how many Modules there are or which Topics belong to each Module.
- **Terminology**: Chaoshui insists on using the term "Lectures" to describe teaching content, which doesn't align with our desired approach to pedagogy.
- **Concept Pages as Files**: Chaoshui is naming concept pages as .pdf or .mp4 files and embedding those directly into the page. While technically functional, the current setup misses the opportunity to unpack images, graphics, and flow charts further to enrich the content. This defeats the purpose of writing course content directly on the page rather than simply embedding a PDF.
- **Accessibility Issues**: The PDFs I've seen are inaccessible, with text embedded as static images that don't work with screen readers.

‌

To Chaoshui's credit, he intends to minimise clicks for students and create a seamless experience. However, achieving this requires thoughtful content development rather than simply uploading the bare minimum.

‌

‌

‌

For **Module 1**, I've restructured it by:

- Renaming the Topic Overview page appropriately.
- Moving Chaoshui’s "Lecture" video to a separate page and uploading it to Panopto for easier captioning and searchable content.
- Unpacking the lecture PDF into a "concept" page called **Technical Contents**, with the same nine headings clearly presented.

‌

I’ve used Ai to flesh out wording for headings and images, (**not** to replace Chaoshui’s writing or expertise, only as a way to showcase the format and layout of the content that he could potentially write and produce himself) and provided instructional design advice in yellow highlight on the page, example below:

‌

‌

‌

Chaoshui suggested adding information about the course overview, CLOs and assessments etc in the **MA2026\_Course\_Outline.pdg**. However, I believe this would duplicate the content already presented on the CurV pages and likely confuse learners.

‌

After your and my meeting at approx. 11:30, I spent about 2-3 hours reorganising and structuring this content and wriing this email. cards.desc:
### Course summary: Enterprise Resource Management (ERM)

- **Program**: Master of Information Technology (Enterprise Management)
- **Course type**: Postgraduate Core
- **Academic year**: 2026 | **AQF Level**: 9 | **Unit Values**: 6
- **Pre-requisite**: Business Process Management

### Course aim

This course introduces enterprise systems (ES), primarily focusing on ERP concepts and how ES can support ERP in Industry 4.0 and the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT). Students work with SAP, managing business processes and making operational decisions in a team-based environment.

### Course structure

- **Module 1**: ES/ERP concepts, business processes, and organisational structures.
- **Module 2**: SAP system use (Material Management, CRM, etc.) from an end-user perspective.
- **Module 3**: ERP system implementation, including business process re-engineering, project management, and change management.

### Learning outcomes

1. Apply ERP principles to real-world cases.
2. Develop ERP solutions for enterprise weaknesses.
3. Critically evaluate tools for business process re-engineering within SAP environments.
4. Use data analytics with ERP for enhanced decision-making.

### Assessments

- **Continuous assessments** (20%): Practicals in SAP.
- **Assignment 1** (20%): Case study analysis.
- **Assignment 2** (20%): Group implementation plan and presentation.
- **Final exam** (40%): Closed book, assessing ERP scenarios.

### Pedagogy

Focuses on active learning, combining asynchronous preparation with in-person seminars and practical SAP sessions.

### Key resources

- **Software**: SAP HANA (educational license), ARIS for business modelling.
- **Readings**: _Integrated Business Information Systems_ (Gronwald, 2020), _Concepts in ERP_ (Monk & Wagner, 2012).

cards.name: Overview
Added Media item to Monday.com for H5P animation

cards.desc: Spent 45 mins
cards.name: Initial meeting

cards.desc:
I've said to the media team:

‌

_"We are requesting that a clickable H5P is transformed into a narrated, step-by-step animation that guides students through each security layer, from 1. Perimeter Security to 10. Backup. The goal is to create a seamless and visually engaging experience that helps reinforce complex security concepts without requiring clicks._

‌

_The content includes audio snippets for each layer, numbered from 1 to 10, provided by the CD. For the animation, we'd like to recreate the static image with enhanced elements, including:_

‌

1. _**Animated Icons and Lines:**_

- _For each layer, relevant icons should have subtle animations to make each point visually distinct. For example, in 1. Perimeter Security, the arrows above the IPS icon could move in a cycle._
- _As each point is introduced, the corresponding orange dotted line can animate (such as a pulsing or directional movement) to illustrate connections between layers._

1. _**Layer Highlights and Emphasis:**_

- _Each shape containing the title of each layer (e.g., 1. Perimeter Security, 2. Network Segmentation) could slowly strobe or pulsate to draw attention as each layer is narrated._
- _When each section’s audio snippet plays, the related layer title and description should appear, reinforcing the content in sync with the narration._

1. _**Engagement and Focus:**_

- _The animation can also feature the orange dotted line moving dynamically to show the connections between different layers, emphasising the layered approach to security._
- _Visual elements should enhance the connections and flow between layers, reinforcing concepts like how network segmentation limits lateral movement with VLANs and Layer 3 switches or how perimeter security with a firewall acts as the first line of defence._

_The aim is to improve engagement by providing a visually connected experience while students listen to the narrated explanations. This would ideally eliminate the need for clicking, which could make understanding the content more intuitive and continuous."_

cards.desc:

‌

These updates were prompted by feedback from the media team, who identified opportunities to improve the pacing, engagement, and alignment of the animation with the voiceover.

‌

Because you were effectively reading the wording in the H5P verbatim, the original script would likely result in some long sections where little to no animation would have occurred, which could have impacted the viewing experience. 

‌

I’ve also prepared a reworked "storyboard" document that includes a revised script and detailed animation notes, incorporating ideas from your PPTX slide notes for each "layer". 

‌

I’d like to share this with you for review to ensure the changes align with your original vision for the content. (Sorry to make you double up on work, but it will likely mean re-doing the voice-over recording to align with the reworked script.)

‌

_In short, if you could please ensure the "script/wording" best aligns with how you envision each part of the animation would playout on the screen for students in this revised table format that would be a massive help for the media team to help interpret and enact on our goal here._

‌

cards.name: Overview

cards.desc:
Hi Nick,

Thanks for reaching out. As we’re now in **Week 9 of Cycle 3**, we’ve technically moved beyond the **Design (Weeks 2–5)** and **Develop (Weeks 4–8)** phases. Incorporating detailed feedback on the mapping at this stage risks delaying your progress, especially as you’re already focused on the Canvas build. Just as an FYI for future cycles, in case there’s any confusion or you weren’t aware, whilst I’m happy to give feedback, it is actually the role of the Curriculum Education Developer to provide assistance with the Assessment design and Course Mapping process, (who was \@Sasikala Rathnappulige) not myself as the Learning Designer. 

For all intents and purposes, well done on the significant work on your mapping, I only wanted to provide feedback on the **overall** **structure and extension weeks**. I’m also happy to help put some basic templates into canvas under the **Assignments** page, if you like?

‌

**Structure and Progression**

‌

The course structure seems to progress logically, starting with foundational topics like ethics and AI/ML history before moving into advanced concepts like bias, transparency, and security.

‌

According to the [course development essential site](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/370/pages/course-delivery-model?module_item_id=3373 "‌"), the _**Course Delivery Model**_ specifies that extension weeks should not introduce new content but instead focus on reflection, assessment, and consultation. I just wanted to highlight that the weekly structure _seems_ to span all 13 weeks, implying that structured teaching occurs during both teaching and extension weeks. It might be worth simplifying what you’ve done at a bird’s eye level and creating a course timeline table that perhaps goes a bit more granular and clearly shows the correlation between Curriculum Models, Teaching weeks, Topics and Assessment due dates?

‌

Below is an example from another course. You’re welcome to create a table and replicate this concept, or I’m happy to help, but I would need your assistance in picking out the correct info from the Mapping doc. Whilst this isn’t a requirement, we’re finding that (including this sort of table will a) benefit the students when the course is run and b) make it easier to create the course structure in the LMS in the [Modules](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1592/modules "‌") tab)

‌

‌

![Image](cid:3965453936*image003.png@01DB4A2D.E9533610)

‌

**Alignment of Extension Weeks with Modules**:

‌

You’ve outlined the three curriculum modules on page 2 with subsequent topics in bullet points; I just wanted to sense check and ask if these modules align with the table further down the page (starting from page 11)?

‌

Apologies if I’m overlooking something, but I see you’ve mentioned the idea of “Human factors and affordances” however I can’t see mention of either of those specific words/concepts further down in the course break down table.

‌

‌

![Image](cid:3965453936*image002.png@01DB4A2B.0AA97B10)

‌

**Assessment Templates** 

‌

As you mentioned you’re fine with the canvas build, I just wanted to offer my help to import some basic templates and create your assignment groups with weighting etc so that you have a structure to work from.

I can do something like below with your approval:

‌

‌

‌

![Image](cid:3965453936*image005.png@01DB4A2F.FBBA23C0)

‌

![Image](cid:3965453936*image004.png@01DB4A2F.C599C930)

‌

I hope this helps and look forward to hearing from you.

‌

Kindly,

‌

**Rich Bartlett**
Learning Designer 

**Online Programs Team | Learning Enhancement & Innovation**
Division of Academic and Student Engagement

‌

**THE UNIVERSITY OF ADELAIDE**
Room 661, Level 6, Kenneth Wills Building
T: [+61 0467 982 427](tel:+61467982427 "‌") 
E: [rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au](mailto:rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au "‌")

cards.desc:
Hi Thilini,

Thanks for sending through the documents and outline for the Construction Science & Materials course. I’ve uploaded the modules and topics, along with the concept pages, into the Canvas course shell. You can access them here: [Canvas Course Shell](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1038/modules "‌").

‌

**Summary of updates and questions:**

1. **Content Extraction**: I've extracted as much content as possible from the AU Curriculum Module & Topic Template Topic 1 document. Based on the structure, my understanding is that most text and teaching content should go into the ["Overview and classification of construction materials](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1038/modules/items/14316 "‌")" concept page. Could you clarify which content should go into the "[Building Systems](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1038/modules/items/49121 "‌")" and "[Selection of Construction Materials](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1038/modules/items/49122 "‌")" concept pages, or are these pages still a work in progress?
2. **Forum Placement**: The student "forum" has been added as a [discussion page,](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/1038/modules/items/49148 "‌") positioned in the teaching sequence following the "Overview and classification of construction materials."

‌

**Feedback on the "Overview and classifications of construction materials" PowerPoint:**

‌

Thanks for the swift turnaround on this presentation. I can see the thought you've put into structuring the content. As a video presentation, however, there are a few areas that could be enhanced to align more closely with multimedia learning principles to improve learner engagement:

‌

- I would suggest reworking the slide notes into a narrative script which can either be read by yourself or used as a guide for a future academic to read and follow so that they may record/create this video closer to when the course will be taught. This will also give the media team the time to uplift the graphic content and then this future academic can simply speak over the top of an enhanced graphics then the video will be good to go. (Please see the attached doc as an example of how I think this script could written)
- You’ve done a great job adding visuals/images. If you have time and capacity, I would suggest having a look at slides 3 & 8 for example to see if there’s any images/graphics which could be used to help convey the teaching.

‌

I’m here to assist with implementing these changes if needed and happy to clarify via a teams meeting this week.

‌

Let me know if there’s anything specific you’d like further feedback on before moving to the next topic.

‌

Cheers!

‌

[https://docs.google.com/document/d/1HcOs\_QWfi5YJ\_dXujsr1gkeszJt4UWKzdt4nlHUvxMk/edit?tab=t.0](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1HcOs_QWfi5YJ_dXujsr1gkeszJt4UWKzdt4nlHUvxMk/edit?tab=t.0 "‌")

**Trello Card Summary**

**Title:** Adelaide University Applied Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Course Development - Feras' Support and Timeline

**Description:**

- Mitzi Jozeps from the Curriculum team (Integration Management Office) is Feras' key contact for the course development of Adelaide University Applied Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning.
- Course development is scheduled from **9 September to 15 November 2024**.
- Key contacts during development include Learning Designer (Richard Bartlett), Curriculum Developer (Bopelo Boitshwarelo), Media Design, Media Production, Course Builder, and the Library for course readings.
- A 10-week Course Developer Journey will guide progress.

**Key Dates & Resources:**

- **Onboarding:** Week 1 starting **9 September 2024**.
- **Assessment & Course Design Check-In:** Week 3 (**23 September 2024**).
- **Content Draft Check-In:** Week 6 (**14 October 2024**).
- **Course Build Check-In:** Week 8 (**28 October 2024**).
- **Course Completion Check-In:** Week 10 (**11 November 2024**).

**Key Support Contacts (Available Dates):**

- **Curriculum Developer:**
  Bopelo Boitshwarelo – 5 hours of support available from **16 September 2024 to 4 October 2024** (Weeks 2-4).
  [Email Bopelo](mailto:Bopelo.Boitshwarelo@unisa.edu.au "‌")
- **Learning Designer:**
  Richard Bartlett – 15 hours of support available from **30 September 2024 to 1 November 2024** (Weeks 4-8).
  [Email Richard](mailto:rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au "‌")
- **Media Design:**
  5 hours of support available from **30 September 2024 to 15 November 2024** (Weeks 4-10).
  [Email Media Design to schedule consult](mailto:mediaproduction@adelaide.edu.au "‌")
  [More Information and Help Guide](https://www.adelaide.edu.au/learning/media-design-guide "‌")
- **Media Production:**
  15 hours of support available from **14 October 2024 to 15 November 2024** (Weeks 6-10). Media Design consult will guide next steps.
- **Course Builder:**
  Available from **14 October 2024 to 15 November 2024** (Weeks 6-10).
- **Library Support:**
  Available from **16 September 2024 to 4 October 2024** (Weeks 2-4) or as needed, recommended to engage early.
  [Email Library Support](mailto:library@adelaideuni.edu.au "‌")

**Next Steps:**

- Visit [AU Course Development Essentials Site](https://adelaide.sharepoint.com/sites/CourseDevelopmentEssentials "‌") for key activities and templates.
- Ensure access to the [Course Shell](https://lms.adelaide.edu.au "‌") and the [SharePoint Folder](https://adelaide.sharepoint.com/sites/CourseDevelopmentEssentials "‌").
- Attend live and asynchronous training in the [AU Canvas Learning Centre](https://www.adelaide.edu.au/canvas-training "‌").

**Milestone Check-In Meetings:**

- Week 3: w/c **23 September 2024** (Assessment & Course Design).
- Week 6: w/c **14 October 2024** (Content Draft).
- Week 8: w/c **28 October 2024** (Course Build).
- Week 10: w/c **11 November 2024** (Course Completion).

**Additional Info:**

- Live training available in Weeks 2, 3, 5, and 7. Book workshops as needed.
  [Register for Workshops](https://www.adelaide.edu.au/learning/workshops "‌")

Hi Richard 

Hi Richard

I hope you're doing well. 

I was wondering if you could take a look at the Canvas site for the Electrical and Electronic Engineering Design  (EEED) course and provide any feedback you might have. This is a relatively new course, so we’re currently focused on ensuring it meets the minimum requirements.

If you could also suggest what those minimum requirements should entail for the course site, it would be very helpful.

Kind regards,
_**Vanika Sharma**_

_Lecturer, Power Engineering_ 

_UniSA STEM_

_University of South Australia, Mawson Lakes, SA 5095_

_Mob: 0452585065| Email:vanika.sharma@_[_unisa.edu.au_](mailto:shavy027@mymail.unisa.edu.au "‌")_ | LinkedIn: _[_linkedin.com/in/vanika-sharma-b51b4630_](https://url.au.m.mimecastprotect.com/s/Cr8mCvl14zh6X9yqFXhgHQ__fj?domain=apac01.safelinks.protection.outlook.com "‌")

cards.desc:
Dear Thomas, Anton & Simon,

‌

As you are aware, **Organic Chemistry 2** has been scheduled for additional development between from **8 September 2025 to 14 November 2025** Following on from the peer review and finalisation period, the following items are to be completed by the end of this additional development period: 

‌

- **CDF and assessment completed** 
- **Rework on front landing page** 
- **Development of video contents** 
- **Contents development for topic 2-6**
- **Build course readings**
- **Restructuring on assessment grouping**

‌

As your Project Officer, I will meet with you periodically throughout the 10-week development cycle to discuss progress. 

‌

**Additional Development Discussion** 

Week 1: 9 September 2025 

**Milestone 1 check-in** 

Week 4: 1 October 2025 

**Milestone 2 check-in** 

Week 7: 21 October 2025 

**Milestone 3 check-in** 

Week 10: 12 November 2025 

_(If you are aware of any leave or scheduled absences during the above weeks, please let me know ASAP.)_ 

‌

**Key Contacts:** 

The TALAS support available during this additional development period is outlined below. The timeline of support is the same as standard course development, but the time allocation has reduced. 

‌

**Role** 

**Name** 

**Email** 

 **Support Provided** 

 **Time Allocation** 

**Available Timeframe** 

**Learning Designer** 

‌

**(LD)** 

Richard Bartlett 

[rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au](mailto:rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au "‌") 

Provides advice and support in the conceptualisation, design, and sequencing of course content and learning activities. They can help you scaffold learning activities toward assessment, scope and design the development of learning content, and provide advice and support in the effective use of technology in learning and teaching. 

‌

7\.5 hours 

**Weeks 4 to 8:** 

‌

Monday 29 September – Friday 31 October 

‌

\*Please note, both your CED & LD are available during week 4 

**Course Build Support** 

Your Learning Designer will arrange course build support 

Builds some content in the LMS; completion of fundamental ad-hoc design tasks. 

‌

10 hours 

**Weeks 6 to 10:** 

‌

Monday 13 October – Friday 14 November 

‌

**Media Design** 

\-Email [jeremy.bowtell@unisa.edu.au](mailto:jeremy.bowtell@unisa.edu.au "‌") to schedule your consult 

 
More information [here.](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/370/pages/media-2 "‌") 

Provides advice and support in selecting types of multimedia for your course, exploring options for filming videos, scheduling filming or media development, and designing bespoke media assets. 

‌

2\.5 hours 

‌

**Weeks 4 to 10:** 

‌

Monday 29 September – Friday 14 November 

**Media Production** 

Your consult with Media Design will guide any next steps 

Supports developers to produce engaging media content (through their team of skilled multimedia designers, filmmakers, animators, recording studios, self-serve facilities and equipment).    

7\.5 hours 

**Weeks 6 to 10:** 

‌

Monday 13 October – Friday 14 November 

‌

**Library** 

(course readings and resource selection) 

[library@adelaideuni.edu.au](mailto:library@adelaideuni.edu.au "‌") 

Support in identifying resources that align with your course. Copyright guidance. 

As needed 

**Recommended in Weeks 2 to 4:** 

‌

Monday 15 September – Friday 3 October  

**Academic Transition Panel**  

**(ATP)** 

Andrew MacKinnon (UoA), Emma Sherratt (UoA), Tom Raimondo (UniSA), & Craig Williams (UniSA) 

Discipline specific academic leads for AU Course Development. 

They provide the programmatic approach and are the contact for Course Aim, CLO amendment requests or workload concerns.  

‌

‌

‌

‌

**Links and Helpful Resources** 

Resource 

Description 

Link 

**Course Shell Link** 

This is the course shell where you will be building out the content. 

[Course Shell Link**.**](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/749 "‌") 

**Course Development Journey** 

Outlines the key activities across the 10 weeks. 

[Course Developer Journey](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/370/pages/key-information "‌") 

**AU Course Development Essentials Site** 

The main hub for course development information. Provides guidance on the design, development, and build phases. 

[Adelaide University Course Development Essentials site](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/370 "‌") 

‌

**SharePoint Folder** 

The dedicated folder for your course in the AU Course Development SharePoint. This holds your documents related to course development and is a collaborative space for you, other developers and assigned TALAS team members.  

[Part 3: Course Development (sharepoint.com)](https://adelaideuni.sharepoint.com/sites/aupcd/SitePages/Part-3--Course-Development.aspx "‌") (you can search by course name) 

‌

**Training & Support** 

‌

_We strongly encourage you to attend training sessions and workshops and view the asynchronous training available to you to support you in the design and build of your course._ 

**Training Hub:** The AU Canvas Learning Centre Training Hub contains links to live and asynchronous training resources. 

[Training Hub](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/209 "‌") 

‌

**Library:** a dedicated team is available to support you in selecting content for your Reading List and using the Course Readings tool.  

Find out more about the library [here](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/370/pages/library?module_item_id=665199 "‌") 

‌

You can contact the library at [library@adelaideuni.edu.au](mailto:library@adelaideuni.edu.au "‌") 

**Course Design workshop:** This 2-hour online design workshop will outline course design and allow time for small group activities to work on developing your course rubric and assessments. 

The Course Design workshops will be held on the following dates: 

- Week 1: Thursday 1pm - 3pm  

- Week 1: Friday 10am - 12pm 

‌

Register for the Course Design workshop [here](https://url.au.m.mimecastprotect.com/s/t0uWCP7LD9SKMRnp3I0hytx-g7G?domain=events.humanitix.com "‌") 

**Workshops:** Access to register for various workshops, such as Canvas, Library, Media, Assessment Building. 

[Workshops](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/370/pages/workshops-2 "‌") 

‌

I look forward to seeing you at our _Additional Development Discussion_ meeting.  

‌

‌

Kind Regards,

‌

**Eileen Cheong** (she/her)

Project Officer | Program & Course Development | Curriculum

Dear Tak, Chris, Hugh and Simon,

‌

Welcome to the official development period for **Nuclear and Radiation Chemistry 2** course, scheduled for development from **30 June 2025 – 5 September 2025**

‌

Since the discipline kick-off meeting a couple of weeks ago, I am your Project Officer within the Curriculum team in the Integration Management Office (IMO) and am your key contact during course development. My support will include ensuring that you have access to everything you need and are aware of all the resources you have available to you. I will meet with you periodically throughout the 10-week development cycle to discuss your progress against the key development milestones (dates below). 

‌

**Milestone 1 check-in:** Assessment and Course Design

Week 1: w/c 30 June 2025

**Milestone 2 check-in:** Course Development

Week 4: w/c  21 July 2025 

**Milestone 3 check-in:** Course Development

Week 7: w/c 11 August 2025

**Milestone 4 check-in:** Course Development Completion

Week 10: w/c 1 September 2025

_(If you are aware of any leave or scheduled absences during the above weeks, please let me know ASAP.)_

‌

**Links and Helpful Resources**

Resource

Description

Link

**Course Shell Link**

This is the course shell where you will be building out the content.

[Course Shell Link](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/6713 "‌")

‌

Please note that Chrome is the preferred browser for using Canvas

**Course Development Journey**

Provides guidance on how to structure your work across the 10 weeks and I will refer to this in our milestone check-ins.

[Course Developer Journey](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/370/pages/course-development-key-information#development-process "‌")

**AU Course Development Essentials Site**

This site outlines key activities, direction for the Design, Development and Build phases and key information on the Adelaide Attainment Model and important templates you must complete. 

[Adelaide University Course Development Essentials site](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/enroll/HNY46D "‌")

**SharePoint Folder**

You have a dedicated folder for your course in the AU Course Development SharePoint. This holds your documents related to course development and is a collaborative space for you, other developers and assigned TALAS team members. Documents saved in SharePoint can be worked on live and will auto-save, allowing you to have visibility of the latest document version, so it is important that you use this folder for all work.

[Part 3: Course Development (sharepoint.com)](https://adelaideuni.sharepoint.com/sites/aupcd/SitePages/Part-3--Course-Development.aspx "‌")

(you can search by course name)

‌

**Training & Support**

‌

_We strongly encourage you to attend training sessions and workshops and view the asynchronous training available to you to support you in the design and build of your course._

**Training Hub:** The AU Canvas Learning Centre Training Hub contains links to live and asynchronous training resources.

[Training Hub](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/209/pages/training-hub "‌")

‌

**Library:** a dedicated team is available to support you in selecting content for your Reading List and using the Course Readings tool. 

Book an appointment [here](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/370/pages/help-and-booking-consultations "‌")

‌

**Trello Card Summary**

**Title:** Adelaide University Foundations of Human Resource Management Course Development - Subha's Support and Timeline

**Description:**

- Kate Bacon from the Curriculum team (Integration Management Office) is Subha's key contact for the course development of Adelaide University Foundations of Human Resource Management.
- Course development is scheduled from **9 September to 15 November 2024**.
- Key contacts during development include Learning Designer (Richard Bartlett), Curriculum Developer (Rebecca Smith), Media Design, Media Production, Course Builder, and the Library for course readings.
- A 10-week Course Developer Journey will guide progress.

**Key Dates & Resources:**

- **Onboarding:** Week 1 starting **9 September 2024**.
- **Assessment & Course Design Check-In:** Week 3 (**23 September 2024**).
- **Content Draft Check-In:** Week 6 (**14 October 2024**).
- **Course Build Check-In:** Week 8 (**28 October 2024**).
- **Course Completion Check-In:** Week 10 (**11 November 2024**).

**Key Support Contacts (Available Dates):**

- **Curriculum Developer:**
  Rebecca Smith – 5 hours of support available from **16 September 2024 to 4 October 2024** (Weeks 2-4).
  [Email Rebecca Smith](mailto:rebecca.smith01@adelaide.edu.au "‌")
- **Learning Designer:**

cards.desc:
This email outlines the 10-week course development process for the Adelaide University Food Quality and Regulation course, from 18 November 2024 to 7 February 2025. Bec McLachlan, Project Officer within the Curriculum team, will serve as the primary contact and will facilitate access to resources, schedule milestone check-ins, and coordinate support.

**Key Support Contacts and Responsibilities:**

- **Curriculum & Education Developer (Brooke Osborne):** Provides curriculum planning and pedagogical advice for course structure, assessment, and design (5 hours during Weeks 2-5).
- **Learning Designer (Richard Bartlett):** Designs media and learning activities, extending the course plan (15 hours during Weeks 4-8). Also coordinates course build support (20 hours during Weeks 6-10).
- **Media Design:** Guides media selection and preparation for development (5 hours from Weeks 4-10).
- **Media Production:** Supports media content production (15 hours during Weeks 6-10).
- **Library Support:** Assists with identifying resources and copyright guidance as needed (Weeks 2-4).

**Onboarding and Resources:**

- **Course Development Essentials Site:** Details design, development, and build activities and includes the Adelaide Attainment Model and CurV templates.
- **Course Shell and SharePoint Folder:** Dedicated spaces for building content and collaborative document storage.

**Training and Workshops:**
Access to live and asynchronous sessions for Canvas, media design, and assessment building, with specific live sessions during Weeks 2, 3, 5, and 7.

**Milestone Check-ins:**
Scheduled at Week 1 (kick-off), Week 4 (assessment and course design), Week 7 (course development progress), and Week 10 (course completion).

Bec encourages reading the entire email for detailed instructions, to attend training sessions, and to confirm any absences that may affect milestone meetings.

cards.desc:
Dear Jonathan, 

‌

Thank you for supporting the development of the new Adelaide University **Negotiation** course, scheduled for development from **13 October 2025 to 19 December 2025.** 

‌

My name is Sophia Carson, I am a Project Officer within the Curriculum team in the Integration Management Office (IMO) and I am your key contact during course development. My support will include ensuring that you have access to everything you need and are aware of all the resources you have available to you. I will meet with you periodically throughout the 10-week development cycle to discuss your progress against the key development milestones (dates below).  

‌

As this course is scheduled for delivery in **Semester 1**, our development period runs **right up to delivery**, with limited time between finalisation and teaching. Please take note of the milestones below - these will help us stay on track so that, by the end of this 10-week period, your course is **ready for review and as close to ready to teach as possible**. Between the later milestones, I’ll also check in via email to monitor progress and provide support as needed. 

‌

**Course kick-off** 

Week 1: w/c 13 October 2025 

**Milestone 1 check-in:**  

- Course Details Form populated 
- Commenced learning content (draft) 
- Commenced media and build planning (draft) 

Week 4: w/c 3 November 2025 

**Milestone 2 check-in:**  

- Canvas built out with relevant modules and topics   
- Completed Learning content and semi-populated in Canvas   
- Assessments built in Canvas 
- Scripts / recordings for videos 
- Visual assets  (H5Ps, images, animations etc.) 

Week 7: w/c 24 November 2025 

**Milestone 3 check-in:**  

- Completed Course Details Form, populate CurV record & export. 
- Canvas course with relevant modules and topics   
- Finalised all learning content   
- Assessments built in Canvas 
- Activities and media embedded   

Week 10: w/c 15 December 2025 

_(If you are aware of any leave or scheduled absences during the above weeks, please let me know ASAP.)_ 

‌

![Clapper board with solid fill, Picture](cid:71299617*fbd7ff4d-bb40-4614-980c-31fcecd794fc)

**Prior to our course kick-off meeting**  

Please ensure that you watch the 5-minute [**Course Development Overview Video**](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/370/pages/program-and-course-overview "‌"). This video provides an overview of what to expect over the next 10 weeks, including the support and resources available to you. Please come to the kick-off meeting with any questions, or feel free to reach out to me beforehand.  

‌

**Key Contacts:** 

During the 10-week Course Development process you have access to support from **TALAS** (an amalgamation of **TIU** - Teaching Innovation Unit and **LEI** - Learning Enhancement and Innovation) and the **Library**. Below are the key contacts for your support, please reach out to book a consult for the weeks available below: 

‌

**Role** 

**Name** 

**Email** 

 **Support Provided** 

 **Time Allocation** 

**Available Timeframe** 

**Curriculum & Education Developer** 

‌

**(CED)** 

Paul Moss 

[paul.moss@adelaide.edu.au](mailto:paul.moss@adelaide.edu.au "‌") 

CEDs provide pedagogical advice and support in curriculum and assessment design. This includes reviewing and providing feedback on course, assignment, and rubric design. 

5 hours 

‌

**Weeks 1 to 4:** 

‌

Monday 13 October – Friday 7 November 

‌

**Learning Designer** 

‌

**(LD)** 

Richard Bartlett 

[rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au](mailto:rich.bartlett@adelaide.edu.au "‌") 

Provides advice and support in the conceptualisation, design, and sequencing of course content and learning activities. They can help you scaffold learning activities toward assessment, scope and design the development of learning content, and provide advice and support in the effective use of technology in learning and teaching. 

‌

‌

15 hours 

**Weeks 4 to 8:** 

‌

Monday 3 November – Friday 5 December 

‌

\*Please note, both your CED & LD are available during week 4

Your Learning Designer will arrange course build support 

Builds some content in the LMS; completion of fundamental ad-hoc design tasks. 

‌

20 hours

Monday 17 November – Friday 19 December

More information [here.](https://learn.adelaide.edu.au/courses/370/pages/media-2 "‌") 

Provides advice and support in selecting types of multimedia for your course, exploring options for filming videos, scheduling filming or media development, and designing bespoke media assets. 

‌

5 hours

Monday 3 November – Friday 19 December

15 hours

(course readings and resource selection)
